title: FIN-05: File name (snake_[model].html, sonnet fallback) + no-external-deps audit
FIN-04: Wire pause to freeze Ambush timers, wormhole countdown, bonus expirations
FIN-03: Reinforcement consumed on patrol loss (respawn flow)
FIN-02: Reinforcement pickup (cadence 90–150 s, 5-cap, squad-marker HUD row)
P5-10: 'SECTOR CLEARED' win condition (patrol covers all reachable cells)
P5-09: S10 soft cap visual handling
P5-08: Phase 5 patrol-loss respawn (current sector, lose held bonuses, keep Scavenger Training)
P5-07: Wormhole entry → sector transfer (persist score/length/upgrade/held bonuses)
P5-06: Wormhole placement (empty cells ≥ 8 apart, retry on failure)
P5-05: 30 s wormhole countdown + 'Next wormhole: NN s' HUD display
P5-04: Wormhole data model + render (implementer chooses readable transition marker)
P5-03: Tempo-multiplier formula per sector + cap
P5-02: Bunker density formula per sector + cap
P4-06: Sprint boost bonus (held, Shift to activate, 3 s charge)
P4-05: Supply / bonus spawn rejection against bunkers
P4-04: Bunker ↔ patrol collision (bunker loses patrol, Cover absorbs)
P4-02: Bunker placement (6% density, rejection sampling)
P4-01: Bunker data model + render (sandbag-gray rounded squares)
P3-06: Ambush ↔ Cover interaction (absorb hit, no cut)
P3-05: Cover bonus (held, HUD icon, absorb on collision)
P3-04: Cut-at-strike-point math (head-side survives, casualties drop)
P3-03: Ambush sweep animation (0.4 s edge-to-edge)
P3-02: Ambush telegraph render (1.5 s translucent gunmetal sight line)
P2-06: Verify §6.6 Acceptance Criteria
description:
# P2-06 — Verify §6.6 Acceptance Criteria

## Summary
Whole-Phase-2 verification pass. Walk every checklist item in PRD §6.6 by hand in the browser. Capture findings; on failure file a bug bead and don't close until resolved.

Per PRD §6.6 acceptance criteria.

## Persona
Primary: Developer.
Secondary: End User (criteria validate user-facing Phase 2 experience).

## Verification Checklist (mirrors §6.6)
- [ ] Camo palette displays (patrol body uses ≥ 3 alternating earth-tone patches; background, supply cache, and field rations all distinct and earth-toned).
- [ ] Patrol head is visually distinguishable from body.
- [ ] Field rations spawn every 20–35 s on a random empty cell.
- [ ] Field rations despawn after 8 s if uncollected.
- [ ] Field rations are worth 5 points; pickup grows the patrol by 1.
- [ ] Phase 1 mechanics (supply cache, wall/self collision, etc.) still work.
- [ ] Scavenger Training upgrade pulls supplies within 1.5 cells of head.
- [ ] A "Phase 2: Dawn Patrol" banner displays on entry.
- [ ] Phase 2→3 transition banner appears on reaching score 150.
- [ ] At most one field-ration drop on screen at a time.

## Per-AC verification method
- Palette: visual inspect at Phase 2 entry; count distinct body tones across segments.
- Head distinguishability: visual; eye dot or notch present.
- Field ration cadence + value: time the spawn, eat one, verify +5 score and +1 length.
- Despawn after 8 s: ignore one, verify it vanishes.
- Phase 1 regression: drive into wall, verify PATROL LOST; collect cache, verify +1 score.
- Scavenger pull: approach a cache without heading directly at it, watch it drift.
- Banners: visual at score 50 and at score 150.
- At most one ration on screen: spawn one (don't pick it up), wait, verify a second doesn't appear until first is gone.

## Persona Mapping
### Developer
- [ ] Each §6.6 item verified; results captured in a comment on this bead before close.

### End User
- [ ] Phase 2 experience matches PRD §6 narrative — dawn breaks, camo unlocks, the patrol learns Scavenger Training and faces gradually escalating tempo on its way to the Phase 3 gate.

## On failure
File via `bd create "P2 AC fail: <which AC>" --type bug --deps "discovered-from:<P2-06 id>"`; keep P2-06 open until resolved.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~200 tokens.
- Implement (verify): ~2 turns, ~1000 tokens (full Phase 2 playthrough).
- Fix iteration buffer: ~0–4 turns, ~0–2000 tokens.
- Total: ~3–7 turns, ~1200–3200 tokens.
- Confidence: medium.

title: P3-01: Ambush data model + spawn cadence (12–20 s)
P2-05: Phase 2→3 gate detection + banner
description:
# P2-05 — Phase 2→3 gate detection + banner

## Summary
When score reaches 150, transition from Phase 2 to Phase 3: set `state.phase = 3`, set `state.phaseName = 'PHASE 3: AMBUSH'`, re-baseline tick rate, trigger a 2-second phase-transition banner. Uses the same `transitionToPhase()` helper introduced by P1-04.

Per PRD §3.1 thresholds; §6.5 gate; §12 gates table.

## Persona
Primary: End User (visible phase escalation).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Extend `checkPhaseGates()` (P1-04) with a Phase-2 branch: when `state.phase === 2 && state.score >= 150`, call `transitionToPhase(3, 'PHASE 3: AMBUSH')`. No new helper needed — reuses P1-04 plumbing. |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] On reaching score 150 in Phase 2, the "PHASE 3: AMBUSH" banner appears for 2 s with fade-in/out.
- [ ] HUD phase text updates to PHASE 3.
- [ ] Score, patrol length, direction, position, held bonuses (Cover and sprint boost don't exist yet in Phase 2 — fine), and any active multiplier all persist across the transition.

### System
- [ ] Phase 2 branch in `checkPhaseGates()` is idempotent (no re-trigger once `state.phase === 3`).
- [ ] `transitionToPhase(3, ...)` triggers a `recomputeTickRate()` so tick rate re-baselines from the new phase floor.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Whole-cloth reuse of `transitionToPhase()` from P1-04.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: collect points up to 150 (use multiplier + field rations to speed up), verify gate triggers correctly.
- Console probe: `state.score = 149; state.score = 150; checkPhaseGates();` — verify the transition fires once.
- Regression: P1-04 Phase-1→2 gate still works.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~100 tokens (trivial).
- Implement: ~0.5 turns, ~200 tokens (one branch).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens.
- Total: ~3 turns, ~600 tokens.
- Confidence: high.

title: P2-04: Score multiplier bonus (2× for 10 s, cadence 60–90 s)
description:
# P2-04 — Score multiplier bonus (2× for 10 s, cadence 60–90 s)

## Summary
Phase-2-onward bonus pickup that activates instantly on pickup (not held). Spawn cadence: uniform random 60–90 s starting at Phase 2 entry. Lifetime on field: 10 s. On pickup, all subsequent supply-cache and field-ration score increments are doubled for 10 seconds. HUD shows an active-multiplier icon with a countdown of remaining seconds during the active period.

Per PRD §10.1 cadence; §10.2 spawn rules; §10.3 (Timed effect, active immediately); §13.4 (HUD shows active multiplier).

## Persona
Primary: End User (a clear power-up moment).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `state.multiplierBonus = null | {x, y, expiresAt}` (on-field instance) and `state.multiplierActive = null | {until}` (active effect). Cadence timer: `state.multiplierNextSpawn = now + rand(60000, 90000)` set on Phase 2 entry and after pickup / despawn. On tick, if no on-field instance and now ≥ scheduled, spawn (rejection-sample). On pickup: set `state.multiplierActive = {until: now + 10000}` and respawn timer. Modify score-increment code (FN-05 cache, P2-02 ration): if `now < state.multiplierActive?.until`, double the increment. Render on-field multiplier with a distinct icon (e.g. a small "×2" overlaid on a coyote-brown circle). HUD: when active, show `×2 (N s)` in the bonuses HUD area (FN-06 placeholder). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] A score-multiplier pickup spawns within ~90 s of Phase 2 entry.
- [ ] On pickup, the next 10 s of supply-cache and field-ration scores are doubled (cache: +2, ration: +10).
- [ ] HUD shows the multiplier as active with a visible countdown (icon + remaining seconds).
- [ ] After 10 s, scoring returns to normal.
- [ ] At most one multiplier on-field at a time; pickups overlap fine (a fresh pickup during an active effect resets the countdown to 10 s — see System spec below).

### System
- [ ] Cadence interval uniform random in [60000, 90000] ms.
- [ ] On-field lifetime is 10 s; uncollected pickups despawn.
- [ ] Active duration is 10 s from pickup time; if picked up while already active, RESET the countdown to 10 s from new pickup time (do not stack durations).
- [ ] Spawn rejection skips cells occupied by patrol, supply cache, field rations, and (eventually) bunkers + other bonuses.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Same time-driven scheduler pattern as field rations (P2-02).
- Active-effect countdown: a single timestamp `until`; rendering subtracts `(until - now) / 1000` to display seconds.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: fudge cadence to 5 s for testing, pick up multiplier, collect a supply cache, verify +2 score. Wait 10 s, collect another, verify +1.
- Manual: pickup multiplier, then pick up another mid-effect — verify countdown resets to 10 s (no stacking).
- Regression: cache + ration scoring still correct when multiplier is inactive.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens.
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1300 tokens (spawn, pickup, score hook, HUD).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~500 tokens.
- Total: ~4 turns, ~2100 tokens.
- Confidence: medium (score hook needs to be plumbed through every score increment — easy to miss one source).

title: P2-03: Scavenger Training upgrade (1.5-cell radius pull on supplies)
description:
# P2-03 — Scavenger Training upgrade (1.5-cell radius pull on supplies)

## Summary
Permanent Phase-2-onward upgrade: each tick, supply caches and field rations within 1.5 cells of the patrol head are pulled toward the head at 0.5 × patrol speed (i.e. they move half a cell per tick toward the head). Pulled cells are still grid-aligned per tick — the pull effect manifests as the cache/ration jumping one grid step closer toward the head every ~2 ticks. Pull is paused during Ambush cut animation (§6.4) — for this bead, the Ambush isn't implemented yet, so this is a hook for later (P3-04 will set a `state.midAmbushCut` flag this bead checks).

Pull applies to caches + rations only — NOT to reinforcements, Cover, multipliers, or sprint boosts.

Per PRD §6.4 (Scavenger Training spec).

## Persona
Primary: End User (most visually obvious upgrade — pickups visibly slide toward the patrol).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `state.scavengerActive = false`, set to `true` on Phase 2 entry (in `transitionToPhase(2, ...)`). Add `scavengerPull()` called from `tick()` AFTER `patrolStep()`. Logic: for each puller-target object (`state.supplyCache`, `state.fieldRation`), compute Chebyshev distance from head; if `dist <= 1.5` (i.e. dist 0 or 1 — head adjacency including diagonals), and not in mid-Ambush-cut, advance the target one cell toward the head every other tick (`state.scavengerCounter` modulo 2 — 0.5× speed). Skip if the proposed cell is occupied by a patrol segment. |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] After Phase 2 entry, the player can see supply caches "drift" toward the patrol when within 1.5 cells of the head.
- [ ] Field rations also drift when within 1.5 cells of the head.
- [ ] Pull rate is visibly slower than patrol motion (about half — one step every ~2 ticks).
- [ ] Reinforcement, Cover, multiplier, and sprint boost pickups do NOT drift toward the patrol (later phases — verify this once those exist).

### System
- [ ] `state.scavengerActive` starts false in Phase 1, true from Phase 2 onward, stays true through Phase 5 (permanent for the run).
- [ ] Pull updates respect cell bounds (won't drift off-grid) and patrol occupancy (won't drift into a patrol segment — skip that tick).
- [ ] Pull pauses when `state.midAmbushCut === true` (hook for P3-04 to honor; harmless no-op until P3-04 lands).

## Patterns to Reuse
- Chebyshev distance: `Math.max(Math.abs(dx), Math.abs(dy))`.
- Half-speed via tick counter modulo 2 (no need for fractional pixels — grid-aligned step).

## Testing Strategy
- Manual (Phase 2): drive close to a supply cache, stop heading toward it, watch it drift onto the head over a few ticks.
- Manual: confirm same behavior with field rations.
- Manual: verify in Phase 1 (set `state.scavengerActive = false` via console) no pull happens — sanity check the gate.
- Regression: pickup-on-collision still works (cache + ration both register).

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~400 tokens (decide pull cadence and the puller list).
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1200 tokens (pull logic + gate).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~500 tokens (manual + edge cases).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~2100 tokens.
- Confidence: medium (pull-onto-patrol-occupied-cell is a subtle edge worth careful test).

title: P2-02: Field rations (cadence, lifetime, score, despawn)
description:
# P2-02 — Field rations (cadence, lifetime, score, despawn)

## Summary
Field rations are a Phase-2-onward bonus pickup themed as a higher-tier supply drop. Spawn cadence: uniform random 20–35 s starting at Phase 2 entry. Lifetime on field: 8 s, then despawn (vanish) uncollected. Pickup value: +5 score, +1 segment. At most one field ration on board at a time (per §3.25). Render in coyote brown (`#8b6f47`) with a small gold accent (`#b8860b`) to read as a higher-tier care package, distinguishable from the supply cache.

Per PRD §3.25 (max 1 on board); §6.3 (spec); §10.1 cadence matrix; §10.2 spawn rules; §13.6 fairness invariants.

## Persona
Primary: End User (bonus pickup chase-or-skip decision).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `state.fieldRation = null | {x, y, expiresAt}` and `state.fieldRationNextSpawn = <ms-timestamp>` (set on Phase 2 entry to `now + rand(20000, 35000)`). On each tick: if `state.phase >= 2` and `state.fieldRation === null` and `now >= state.fieldRationNextSpawn`, spawn one (rejection-sample against patrol and supply cache); set `expiresAt = now + 8000`. If `state.fieldRation !== null` and `now >= expiresAt`, despawn (set to null and schedule next). On patrol head landing on it, `state.score += 5`, `patrol.grow(1)`, set to null and schedule next. Render: coyote brown filled rounded-corner-ish square with a small gold square in one corner. |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] Field rations begin spawning within ~35 s of Phase 2 entry.
- [ ] Picking up a field ration adds 5 to the score and grows the patrol by 1 segment.
- [ ] An uncollected field ration vanishes after 8 s on the field.
- [ ] At most one field ration is on the board at any time.
- [ ] Field rations look distinct from supply caches (different color, optional gold accent — clearly a higher-tier item).

### System
- [ ] Cadence interval is uniform random in [20000, 35000] ms.
- [ ] Spawn rejection skips cells occupied by patrol segments and the active supply cache.
- [ ] When `state.phase === 1`, no field rations spawn (cadence timer doesn't start until Phase 2 entry).
- [ ] After Phase 2 entry, the cadence timer pauses during `state.paused` (FIN-04 wires the freeze; for this bead, OK to leave timer ticking; FIN-04 will fix).

## Patterns to Reuse
- Time-driven scheduler pattern: `state.fieldRationNextSpawn = now + rand(min, max)` set after spawn or despawn.
- Same rejection-sampling helper from FN-05 (extend it to exclude `state.fieldRation` too).

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: reach Phase 2, wait up to 35 s, verify a field ration appears. Eat it, verify score +5, length +1, new cadence starts.
- Manual: spawn a field ration (or wait), don't pick it up, verify it vanishes after ~8 s.
- Regression: supply caches keep working in Phase 2.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens.
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1400 tokens (state, cadence timer, render).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~500 tokens (timing-dependent — may need to fudge cadence to ~3 s for testing).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~2200 tokens.
- Confidence: high.

title: P2-01: Phase 2 camo palette swap on gate (blocky-camo segments, crate-styled supply cache)
description:
# P2-01 — Phase 2 camo palette swap on gate (blocky-camo segments, crate-styled supply cache)

## Summary
On entering Phase 2, swap from grayscale night-recon visuals to the full field-ops camo palette: background `#2b2d24`, subtle gridlines `#353727`, patrol body segments alternate (per index) among olive `#556b2f` / tan `#c2b280` / coyote brown `#8b6f47` / black `#1a1a14` so the body reads as blocky camouflage, patrol head darker olive `#3b4a1f` with a directional notch or eye-dot for orientation, supply cache rendered as a tan crate (`#c2b280` fill, `#4a3823` border, optional 1px strap line). The palette stays in effect from Phase 2 through Phase 5 (no jarring swaps later).

Per PRD §1.3 aesthetic; §3.14 visual language; §6.2 palette hex table; §13.7 polish AC.

## Persona
Primary: End User ("dawn breaks" — the most visible single moment in the run).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Extend `palette()` (P1-01) Phase ≥ 2 branch: returns the camo color set. Replace placeholder patrol render with a per-segment loop where segment `i` uses `BODY_TONES[i % BODY_TONES.length]` (BODY_TONES is an array of 4 earth tones in the order olive / tan / coyote / black). Head uses head color and draws a small directional notch (a 4×4 px filled rect at the leading edge of the head, in a darker tone) OR an eye dot. Supply cache draws as a filled tan square with a darker border 2 px inset, plus an optional 1 px horizontal strap. |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] On reaching score 50, the visuals swap from grayscale to camo palette immediately after the phase banner fades (or coincident with it).
- [ ] Patrol body shows at least 3 alternating earth-tone patches across consecutive segments — clearly reads as camouflage, not gradient or single color.
- [ ] Patrol head is visually distinguishable from body (different color AND directional cue — notch or eye-dot pointing in heading direction).
- [ ] Supply cache reads as a small crate or ammo box (tan fill, dark outline, not a fruit / candy / gem).
- [ ] Background is dark olive (`#2b2d24`) with very subtle gridlines (barely visible — terrain texture).
- [ ] No neon or saturated colors appear.

### System
- [ ] `palette()` returns Phase ≥ 2 palette when `state.phase >= 2`.
- [ ] Per-segment body tone is deterministic on segment index (so the camo pattern is stable; not flickering per frame).

## Patterns to Reuse
- Phase-branch in `palette()` set up by P1-01.
- Hex values come straight from PRD §6.2 table — no improvisation needed.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: play through to Phase 2 gate, watch the swap. Confirm body is camo and head is distinguishable.
- Manual: collect a supply cache, verify it reads as a crate (not a circle or fruit-styled).
- Regression: Phase 1 grayscale still works (palette branches don't bleed).

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~200 tokens (re-read §6.2 palette).
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1200 tokens (palette extension + per-segment render + crate render).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~400 tokens (play to gate + visual check).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~1800 tokens.
- Confidence: high.

title: P1-05: Verify §5.5 Acceptance Criteria
description:
# P1-05 — Verify §5.5 Acceptance Criteria

## Summary
Whole-Phase-1 verification pass. Walk every checklist item in PRD §5.5 by hand in the browser and confirm each. Capture findings; if any AC fails, file a child bug bead (`discovered-from:<this-bead-id>`) and DO NOT close P1-05 until the failing AC is resolved.

This is the gate item for closing the Phase 1 epic (EP-02).

Per PRD §5.5 acceptance criteria.

## Persona
Primary: Developer (verification work).
Secondary: End User (the criteria validate the user-facing experience).

## Verification Checklist (mirrors §5.5)
- [ ] Patrol spawns at center, length 3, heading right, on game start.
- [ ] Patrol does not move until first directional input.
- [ ] Arrow keys and WASD both control movement.
- [ ] Direction reversal protection blocks instant 180° turns.
- [ ] A supply cache spawns on a random empty cell.
- [ ] Picking up a supply cache increments score by 1 and grows the patrol by 1 segment.
- [ ] Score displays in top-left HUD.
- [ ] Wall collision loses the patrol.
- [ ] Self-collision loses the patrol.
- [ ] Speed ramps from 6 to 8 cells/sec as score climbs from 0 to 50.
- [ ] Phase 1→2 transition banner appears on reaching score 50.
- [ ] Visual palette is grayscale (light-gray patrol on near-black background, no colored elements).

## Per-AC verification method
- Spawn / heading / wait-for-input: visual at page load.
- Arrow + WASD: try each direction with each control scheme.
- Reversal protection: head right, mash Left arrow — verify patrol doesn't reverse.
- Cache spawn + score + grow: drive into cache, observe.
- HUD score: visual top-left.
- Wall collision: drive into each edge.
- Self-collision: grow to ≥ 5 segments, turn into body.
- Speed ramp: feel for the change between score 0 and 50; spot-check via console `state.tickRate`.
- Gate banner: collect 50 score, watch banner.
- Grayscale: confirm no colors.

## Persona Mapping
### Developer
- [ ] Each checklist item in PRD §5.5 individually verified in the browser; results captured in a brief comment on this bead before close.

### End User
- [ ] The whole-Phase-1 experience plays the way PRD §5 describes: a familiar Snake under night-recon mood that escalates to a 6→8 speed ramp and gates into Phase 2 on score 50.

## On failure
If any AC item fails, file a follow-up bug via:
`bd create "P1 AC fail: <which AC>" --type bug --deps "discovered-from:<P1-05 id>"`
Then keep P1-05 open until the bug is fixed and the AC verifies.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~200 tokens (open the file + read §5.5).
- Implement (verify): ~2 turns, ~800 tokens (play through the checklist).
- Fix iteration buffer: ~0–3 turns, ~0–1500 tokens (if any AC fails, file a bug bead and re-verify).
- Total: ~3–6 turns, ~1000–2500 tokens.
- Confidence: medium (depends on whether bugs surface during verification).

comments.author: Robert Teller
comments.text:
Implementation of FN-01..FN-08 and P1-01..P1-04 landed in examples/snake_opus.html (513 lines, single-file, no external deps).

Static / structural verification done:
- HTML structure parses; node --check on the extracted JS reports OK (no syntax errors).
- External-dep audit: grep -Ein 'src=|href=|@import|cdn|http' returns nothing.
- Canonical copy strings present and exact: 'PATROL LOST', 'Press R to redeploy', 'PHASE 1: NIGHT RECON', 'PHASE 2: DAWN PATROL', 'HOLD POSITION'.
- Constants match PRD spec: GRID_W=32, GRID_H=24, CELL=20, BASE_TICK_RATE=6, PHASE1_GATE_SCORE=50.
- Recompute-tick-rate formula matches §3.16: min(6 + floor(score/25), 8) in Phase 1.

NOT verified in this session: live browser playthrough of each §5.5 AC item (the running environment has no browser surface).

Status: leaving P1-05 OPEN as a needs-browser-verification gate. Next session with browser access (or a playwright-mcp surface) should run through the §5.5 checklist and either close P1-05 or file follow-up bugs via 'bd create ... --deps discovered-from:SNAKE-egj.5'.

title: P1-04: Phase 1→2 gate detection + banner
description:
# P1-04 — Phase 1→2 gate detection + banner

## Summary
When score reaches 50, transition from Phase 1 to Phase 2: set `state.phase = 2`, set `state.phaseName = 'PHASE 2: DAWN PATROL'`, re-baseline tick rate (the formula for Phase 2 is again `6 + floor((score - 50) / 25)` per the standard in-phase ramp), and trigger a 2-second phase-transition banner via FIN-01 (or a local shim if FIN-01 isn't done yet — see Patterns). Score, patrol position, length, direction, and reinforcements all persist per PRD §3.19. The banner appears WITHOUT pausing the game (patrol keeps moving) per §3.37.

This bead lays the gate-detection pattern that P2-05, P3-07, and P4-07 will copy.

Per PRD §3.1, §3.19, §3.37, §5.4, §12.

## Persona
Primary: End User (phase transitions are the visible reward of progress).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `checkPhaseGates()` called after every score change. Phase-1 branch: when `state.phase === 1 && state.score >= 50`, call `transitionToPhase(2, 'PHASE 2: DAWN PATROL')`. `transitionToPhase(n, name)`: sets `state.phase = n`, `state.phaseName = name`, calls `recomputeTickRate()` (P1-03), triggers banner. Banner: if FIN-01 is done, call `showPhaseBanner(name)`; otherwise local 2-second `setTimeout`-driven DOM overlay (acceptable interim). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] On reaching score 50 in Phase 1, a 2-second banner reading "PHASE 2: DAWN PATROL" appears centered over the canvas, then fades.
- [ ] During the banner, the patrol keeps moving (per §3.37 — the game does not pause).
- [ ] After the transition, the HUD phase text reads "PHASE 2: DAWN PATROL" (or equivalent).
- [ ] Score, patrol length, direction, and position are all preserved across the transition (verify: collect a supply at score 49, then the next one immediately triggers transition; patrol does NOT reset).

### System
- [ ] `transitionToPhase()` is generic (takes phase number + display name) so later gates (P2-05, P3-07, P4-07) reuse it.
- [ ] `checkPhaseGates()` is idempotent — once `state.phase === 2`, the Phase-1 gate branch no longer fires.
- [ ] `recomputeTickRate()` is called after phase change so the speed ramp re-baselines from the new phase floor.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Reuse the `palette()` / `recomputeTickRate()` phase-branch pattern.
- Banner display: if FIN-01 isn't landed yet, write a minimal local banner — a centered overlay div with the text, displayed for 2000 ms with a CSS fade (`opacity: 1 → 0`). FIN-01 will generalize.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: collect supply caches up to score 50, verify banner appears, phase HUD text changes, and patrol continues moving.
- Manual: verify Phase 2 visuals (P2-01 controls the palette swap) will activate once P2-01 lands; for this bead alone, the palette stays Phase-1 grayscale until P2-01 wires the swap.
- Regression: Phase 1 gameplay (P1-01..P1-03) still works pre-gate.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (decide `transitionToPhase()` API).
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1200 tokens (gate + transition + banner stub or wire).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~400 tokens (manual score-to-50 run).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~1900 tokens.
- Confidence: high.
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (checkPhaseGates, transitionToPhase, showBanner): on score >= 50 in Phase 1, switches phase + name, recomputes tick rate, displays 2-second fade banner via the .banner element (CSS opacity transition + setTimeout). Patrol position/length/direction/score all persist. Generic transitionToPhase ready for P2-05 / P3-07 / P4-07 reuse.

title: P1-03: Intra-phase speed ramp (6 → 8 cells/sec by score 50)
description:
# P1-03 — Intra-phase speed ramp (6 → 8 cells/sec by score 50)

## Summary
Implement the in-Phase-1 speed ramp: `tickRate` scales with score from 6 cells/sec at score 0 to 8 cells/sec at score 50 (the Phase 1→2 gate). The PRD specifies "one cell/sec per 25 score" (§3.16) so the formula is `tickRate = 6 + floor(score / 25)`, capped at 8 within Phase 1. Phase transitions re-baseline; later phases will extend the formula (P5 sectors multiply on top per §3.11, handled by P5-03).

Per PRD §3.16, §5.3, §12.3.

## Persona
Primary: End User (perceptible difficulty curve).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `recomputeTickRate()` invoked on every score change (e.g., right after the supply cache pickup increments score in FN-05). Phase-1 formula: `state.tickRate = Math.min(6 + Math.floor(state.score / 25), 8)`. Hook on phase entry too (so re-baseline works when phases re-set their base via P2-01 etc.). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] At score 0 in Phase 1, patrol moves at ~6 cells/sec (perceptibly slow).
- [ ] At score 25, patrol moves at ~7 cells/sec (slightly faster).
- [ ] At score 50 (right at the Phase 1→2 gate), patrol moves at ~8 cells/sec.

### System
- [ ] `tickRate` is recomputed on every score increment.
- [ ] In Phase 1, `tickRate` is clamped at 8 — score above 50 (which shouldn't happen before the gate but is possible if the gate fires after score) doesn't push beyond 8 within Phase 1.
- [ ] Other phases will override / extend the formula via their own `recomputeTickRate` branch — this bead establishes the Phase-1 branch only.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Phase-branched formula inside `recomputeTickRate()` — like `palette()`, a single source of truth that switches on `state.phase`.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: collect supply caches up to 25 score, time the difference (subjective: it's perceptibly faster).
- Console probe: set `state.score = 50; recomputeTickRate(); console.log(state.tickRate)` — expect 8.
- Console probe: set `state.score = 100; recomputeTickRate(); console.log(state.tickRate)` while still in Phase 1 — expect 8 (clamped).
- Regression: tick rate base 6 at score 0 still holds; FN-02 loop respects the value.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~200 tokens.
- Implement: ~1 turn, ~500 tokens.
- Test: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (timing + console probes).
- Total: ~3 turns, ~1000 tokens.
- Confidence: high.
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (recomputeTickRate, Phase 1 branch): tickRate = min(6 + floor(score/25), 8). Recomputed after every score increment and on phase transition; phase-branched so P5-03 / P2-05 etc. extend cleanly.

title: P1-02: Wall and self-collision loss
description:
# P1-02 — Wall and self-collision loss

## Summary
After each patrol step, check whether the head landed on (a) a cell outside the grid (wall) or (b) a cell occupied by any non-head patrol segment (self). Either case calls `losePatrol()` (provided by FN-08), which displays the PATROL LOST overlay. Walls kill across all phases per PRD §3.17.

For Phase 1: any loss is terminal (no reinforcements yet; FIN-02 adds them later). For now `losePatrol()` always shows the overlay.

Per PRD §3.17 (walls kill), §4.5 (wall + self lose patrol).

## Persona
Primary: End User (collision death is core feedback).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | After `patrolStep()` (FN-04), add a `checkCollisions()` call that: (a) returns 'wall' if `head.x < 0 || head.x >= 32 || head.y < 0 || head.y >= 24`, (b) returns 'self' if any segment in `segments.slice(1)` matches head, (c) returns null otherwise. On non-null result, call `losePatrol()` (FN-08) and skip further per-tick processing this tick. |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] Driving the patrol into the top, bottom, left, or right edge triggers the PATROL LOST screen.
- [ ] Driving the head into a body segment triggers the PATROL LOST screen.
- [ ] After a collision, the patrol stops moving and the overlay appears immediately (no further ticks).
- [ ] Cells INSIDE the grid (0 ≤ x ≤ 31, 0 ≤ y ≤ 23) do not trigger wall loss.

### System
- [ ] Collision detection runs AFTER `patrolStep()` (head has already advanced) — verifies the post-step head position.
- [ ] Self-collision check uses `segments.slice(1)` (skip head) to avoid false positives.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Simple bounds check + linear scan of segments. At max patrol length (~150 segments late game), linear scan is fine; switch to a Set if profiling demands.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: drive into each wall, verify loss + overlay.
- Manual: grow patrol to ~5 segments, deliberately turn into body, verify self-collision loss.
- Regression: normal movement (no collision) keeps running.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~200 tokens.
- Implement: ~1 turn, ~600 tokens (bounds + segment scan + losePatrol hook).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~400 tokens (4 wall directions + self).
- Total: ~3 turns, ~1200 tokens.
- Confidence: high.
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (checkCollisions, tick): bounds check on grid then linear scan of segments.slice(1) for self-collision; either triggers losePatrol() before any pickup processing this tick.

title: P1-01: Wire grayscale night-recon palette + Phase 1 visuals
description:
# P1-01 — Wire grayscale night-recon palette + Phase 1 visuals

## Summary
Apply the Phase 1 grayscale night-recon styling to all visible elements during Phase 1: near-black background (`#0e0e0e` or dark gray on the canvas, distinct from Phase 2's `#2b2d24` dark olive), light-gray patrol on dark gray gridlines (1px at 10% opacity), supply cache slightly lighter than background. NO colored elements. The grayscale only applies in Phase 1; the Phase 2 gate (P2-01) restores the camo palette. Per PRD §5.2, §13.7.

## Persona
Primary: End User (palette is the immediate visual cue that Phase 1 is the night-recon training mood).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Introduce a `palette()` helper that returns colors based on `state.phase`. Phase 1 returns: `bg='#0e0e0e'`, `gridline='rgba(180,180,180,0.10)'`, `patrolHead='#cccccc'`, `patrolBody='#999999'` (single gray tone — no alternating camo in Phase 1), `supplyCache='#3a3a3a'`. The canvas clear + draws in FN-01..FN-05 must consult `palette()` rather than hardcode colors. Draw subtle gridlines on canvas during render. Patrol body uses a single gray tone for all segments in Phase 1 (alternating earth-tone patches come in P2-01). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] Phase 1 background is near-black (visually distinct from the dark-olive Phase 2 background).
- [ ] Subtle gray gridlines are visible on the canvas at ~10% opacity (not stark, not absent).
- [ ] Patrol renders in a single light-gray tone, head slightly lighter than body (still distinguishable).
- [ ] Supply cache is a flat light-gray-on-dark cell that reads against the terrain.
- [ ] No colored elements appear in Phase 1 (no greens, browns, golds — strictly grayscale).
- [ ] The Phase 1 "night-recon / training-exercise" mood is unambiguous on first frame.

### System
- [ ] `palette()` is a single source of truth — all draw functions consult it rather than hardcoding colors.
- [ ] Switching `state.phase` from 1 to 2 (manually via console for verification) causes the next render to swap to Phase 2 colors (P2-01 will own the actual swap; verifying `palette()` plumbing here).

## Patterns to Reuse
- Switch over `state.phase` inside `palette()`: a `case` per phase. Future phases extend the same helper.
- Gridline drawing: a single loop of vertical + horizontal lines, drawn once per frame on the canvas at low opacity.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: open the page, verify the Phase 1 grayscale mood. Temporarily set `state.phase = 2` in the console, refresh draws, confirm a palette switch is plumbed (Phase 2 visuals are placeholder until P2-01).
- Manual: ensure no neon / saturated colors are visible in Phase 1.
- Regression: FN-01..FN-05 still work; collisions still trigger PATROL LOST etc.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (decide `palette()` shape).
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1200 tokens (palette helper + draws consulting it + gridlines).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (visual check).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~1800 tokens.
- Confidence: high.
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (palette function + renderFrame): Phase 1 branch returns grayscale tones (bg #0e0e0e, gridline rgba(180,180,180,0.10), single gray body, light-gray head, gray cache); Phase 2+ branch returns the camo palette per PRD §6.2 (placeholder until P2-01 refines styling).

title: FN-08: PATROL LOST screen + R/Space redeploy
description:
# FN-08 — PATROL LOST screen + R/Space redeploy

## Summary
Implement the run-end screen. When the patrol is lost AND no reinforcements remain (initially: any loss, since reinforcements come in FIN-02/FIN-03), display the PATROL LOST overlay with the canonical copy: headline "PATROL LOST", "Final Score: N", "Phase reached: P", "Press R to redeploy". On Phase 5 also show sector (`Phase 5 — Sector S`). R or Space (per PRD §3.23) redeploys to a fresh Phase 1 run.

Per PRD §3.21 (canonical screen copy); §3.23 (R only from this screen, ignored mid-game); §13.5 (whole-game AC for the screen); §3.24 SECTOR CLEARED variant is OUT of scope here — handled by P5-10.

## Persona
Primary: End User (run-end is a user-visible state).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `state.runState` enum: `'playing' \| 'patrol_lost' \| 'awaiting_first_input'` (default `'awaiting_first_input'`; flips to `'playing'` on first input; flips to `'patrol_lost'` when the loss handler fires). Add HUD overlay `#hud-lost` with the four canonical lines (PATROL LOST / Final Score / Phase reached / Press R to redeploy) styled per PRD §13.5. Extend FN-03 key handler: only when `runState === 'patrol_lost'`, R or Space triggers `redeploy()`. `redeploy()` resets score, patrol (via FN-04 `patrolReset`), supply cache (via FN-05 `spawnSupplyCache`), sets `runState = 'awaiting_first_input'`, hides overlay. Add a `losePatrol()` function: sets `runState = 'patrol_lost'`, shows the overlay, fills in score / phase / (sector if P5). Other modules call `losePatrol()` on lethal events (P1-02 wires wall + self collision). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] On patrol loss, the overlay shows EXACTLY: "PATROL LOST" (headline), "Final Score: N" (with current score), "Phase reached: P" (with current phase number; "Phase 5 — Sector S" when in Phase 5), "Press R to redeploy" (bottom).
- [ ] Pressing R from the overlay restarts a fresh Phase 1 run (center, length 3, awaiting first input).
- [ ] Pressing Space has the same effect as R from the overlay.
- [ ] Mid-game R is ignored (no restart) per §3.23.
- [ ] Background is visually dimmed under the overlay so the canvas is still partially visible.

### System
- [ ] Overlay copy strings are EXACT — verify "PATROL LOST" not "GAME OVER" or "YOU DIED"; verify "Press R to redeploy" not "Press R to restart".
- [ ] `redeploy()` fully resets score and patrol; phase resets to 1; reinforcements reset to 0; held bonuses cleared (no bonuses exist yet so no-op for now).

## Patterns to Reuse
- DOM overlay with text content set on display (like FN-07's pause overlay).
- Canonical copy strings stored as constants at the top of the script for easy verification.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: trigger a loss (drive into a wall — P1-02 wires that), verify each line of canonical copy is exact, press R, verify fresh run starts.
- Manual: during play, press R, verify it is ignored.
- Regression: FN-01..FN-07 still work.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens.
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1200 tokens (overlay + redeploy + state enum).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~400 tokens (manual loss + redeploy cycles).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~1900 tokens.
- Confidence: high (canonical copy is explicit in PRD §3.21).
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (losePatrol, redeploy, #overlay-lost): exact canonical copy 'PATROL LOST' headline, 'Final Score: N', 'Phase reached: P', 'Press R to redeploy' foot; R or Space accepted only from patrol_lost state (mid-game R ignored per §3.23). SECTOR CLEARED variant deferred to P5-10.

title: FN-07: HOLD POSITION overlay + P/Esc toggle
description:
# FN-07 — HOLD POSITION overlay + P/Esc toggle

## Summary
Implement pause toggle: P or Esc flips `state.paused`. When paused, show a "HOLD POSITION" overlay (centered text, dimmed background). Per PRD §3.22, pause must freeze the tick clock AND all world-event timers (Ambush, wormhole countdown, bonus expirations) — but the world-event timers are wired in later phases. FN-07 establishes the `state.paused` flag and the overlay; FIN-04 wires the timer-freezing for Ambush/wormhole/bonuses.

Per PRD §3.22 (pause behavior); §4.3 (controls P/Esc).

## Persona
Primary: End User (pause is a user-controlled mechanic).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Extend FN-03 key handler: when key is `KeyP` or `Escape`, flip `state.paused`. Add HUD overlay div `#hud-pause` with text "HOLD POSITION", centered, hidden by default. In `renderHud()` (FN-06), toggle the overlay's visibility on `state.paused`. The tick clock freeze is already provided by FN-02 (it skips `tick()` when paused). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] Pressing P while running shows the "HOLD POSITION" overlay and stops patrol movement.
- [ ] Pressing P again resumes (overlay hides, patrol resumes movement, score and game state preserved).
- [ ] Esc has the same effect as P.
- [ ] Overlay is visually distinct (centered text, dimmed background using `rgba(0,0,0,0.5)` or similar).
- [ ] Pause does NOT lose any state (score, patrol length, position).

### System
- [ ] `state.paused` toggles correctly; pause/resume are idempotent (P-press while paused only resumes once).
- [ ] World-event timer freezing for Ambush / wormhole / bonus is OUT of scope here and tracked separately as FIN-04.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Single DOM overlay with display toggle: `pauseEl.style.display = state.paused ? 'flex' : 'none'`.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: press P during gameplay, verify movement halts and overlay appears. Press P again, verify resume. Repeat with Esc.
- Regression: FN-02 loop, FN-03 input, FN-04 patrol movement still work.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~200 tokens (decide overlay style).
- Implement: ~1 turn, ~600 tokens (toggle + overlay).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~200 tokens (manual check).
- Total: ~3 turns, ~1000 tokens.
- Confidence: high.
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (keydown handler + .pause-overlay): P or Esc toggles state.paused; HOLD POSITION overlay with dimmed background appears while paused; FN-02 loop skips tick() during pause. World-event timer freezing (Ambush / wormhole / bonus expirations) deferred to FIN-04 as designed.

title: FN-06: HUD renderer (score, reinforcements, phase, held-bonus icons)
description:
# FN-06 — HUD renderer (score, reinforcements, phase, held-bonus icons)

## Summary
Implement the HUD render layer: draw the score at top-left, the reinforcements row (squad-marker chevrons, max 5) at top-right, and the current phase name in the top-center, per PRD §13.4. Reserve render slots for held-bonus icons (Cover, sprint boost, active multiplier) — these go unused until later phases (P3, P4, P2 respectively) but the structure is in place. The HUD can either be DOM (overlaid divs) or canvas-drawn; DOM is simpler and keeps the canvas focused on the playfield — recommended.

Per PRD §3.21 (PATROL LOST is a separate screen — FN-08 owns it; FN-06 is gameplay HUD only); §3.28 (squad-marker chevrons; heart icon acceptable if chevron unreadable); §13.4 (HUD spec).

## Persona
Primary: End User (HUD is constantly visible during play).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | In `<body>`, add HUD container divs: `#hud-score` (top-left), `#hud-phase` (top-center), `#hud-reinforcements` (top-right), `#hud-bonuses` (below phase). CSS positions them absolutely over the canvas with parchment monospace styling. Add `renderHud()` called from the render loop: writes `state.score`, `state.phaseName` (default "PHASE 1: NIGHT RECON"), and renders `state.reinforcements` (init 0) as N chevron glyphs (Unicode `▼` or `⌃` works; or a small SVG/CSS shape). `#hud-bonuses` is empty for now — P2/P3/P4 will populate. |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] Score (starting "0") is visible top-left and updates when supply caches are collected.
- [ ] Current phase name is visible top-center.
- [ ] Reinforcements area is visible top-right (initially empty since reserve starts at 0; FIN-02 adds chevrons on pickup).
- [ ] HUD text uses parchment/monospace styling consistent with PRD §6.2 (`#e5e0c8`).

### System
- [ ] `renderHud()` is called every render frame (so score updates immediately on cache pickup).
- [ ] HUD never occludes the playfield (`pointer-events: none` on overlay divs).

## Patterns to Reuse
- DOM overlay over canvas: position the canvas in a wrapper `<div>` with `position: relative`, HUD divs `position: absolute` over it with `pointer-events: none`.
- Reinforcement chevrons: a simple flexbox row with N child elements; rebuild on every render (cheap for max 5 items).

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: collect supply caches and verify the score in the HUD updates. Verify phase name is readable. Verify the reinforcements slot is positioned correctly even when empty.
- Regression: canvas playfield (FN-01..FN-05) still renders cleanly.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens.
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1200 tokens (CSS + DOM + render hook).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (manual visual check).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~1800 tokens.
- Confidence: high.
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (renderHud): DOM-overlay HUD (pointer-events: none), parchment monospace color #e5e0c8, top-left score / top-center phase / top-right reinforcements (chevron row reconciled to state.reinforcements). Held-bonus icons region (#hud-bonus) reserved for P2/P3/P4.

title: FN-05: Supply cache spawn (rejection sampling against patrol)
description:
# FN-05 — Supply cache spawn (rejection sampling against patrol)

## Summary
Implement the supply-cache mechanic: at game start, spawn one cache at a random empty cell (rejection-sample against patrol segments). On patrol-head collision with the cache cell, increment score by 1, call `patrol.grow(1)`, and immediately spawn a new cache on the next tick (per PRD §3.34, no delay). Always exactly one cache on board at a time.

Per PRD §3.34 (no respawn delay); §4.1 (score is monotonic); §4.4 (growth +1 on cache); §13.6 (cache never spawns on patrol or bunkers — bunkers come in Phase 4, handled then via P4-05).

## Persona
Primary: End User (cache pickup is the core scoring loop).
Secondary: System (spawn rejection-sampling).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `state.score = 0`. Add `state.supplyCache = {x, y}` or null. Add `spawnSupplyCache()` that picks a random `(x, y)` in the grid where no patrol segment occupies the cell (rejection-sample, max ~100 tries; fall back to first empty cell on exhaustion). Call once at game start. After `patrolStep()`, if `head.x === supplyCache.x && head.y === supplyCache.y`, do `state.score += 1`, `patrol.grow(1)`, then `spawnSupplyCache()`. Render the cache as a placeholder filled cell (P2-01 replaces with crate styling). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] One supply cache is visible on board at game start, not on top of the patrol.
- [ ] Steering the patrol head into the cache increments score by 1 and grows the patrol by 1 segment.
- [ ] A new cache appears immediately on the same tick the old one is consumed.

### System
- [ ] `spawnSupplyCache` never places the cache on a cell currently occupied by a patrol segment.
- [ ] Exactly one cache exists at any time.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Rejection sampling: `do { x = randInt(0, 32); y = randInt(0, 24); } while (patrolOccupies(x, y) && --attempts > 0);` Future bunker/wormhole exclusion will extend the occupancy check via P4-05.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: drive the patrol into the cache, verify score increments, patrol grows, new cache appears.
- Manual edge: run for ~30 s gathering caches, verify length grows monotonically.
- Regression: FN-02..FN-04 still work.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens.
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1200 tokens (spawn + collision + render placeholder).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (manual pickup test).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~1800 tokens.
- Confidence: high.
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (spawnSupplyCache, tick collision branch): rejection sampling against patrol segments (200 attempts + linear-scan fallback), score+1 and grow(1) on head-cache collision, immediate respawn same tick.

title: FN-04: Patrol data model (segments, head, growth, length math)
description:
# FN-04 — Patrol data model (segments, head, growth, length math)

## Summary
Implement the snake/patrol data model: an array of `{x, y}` cell coordinates where index 0 is the head and the tail is at the end. Provide `step(direction)` that advances the head and either truncates the tail (no growth) or keeps it (growth). Provide `grow(n)` to schedule N growth ticks. Provide `reset(cx, cy, length, heading)` for fresh spawn (called by FN-08 / phase respawn). Wire FN-04 into FN-02's `tick()` callback to drive movement, gated on `state.firstInputReceived` per PRD §3.39 for Phase 1.

Per PRD §3.20 (spawn: center, length 3, heading right); §4.4 (growth +1 per supply); §3.39 (Phase 1 waits for first input; respawn auto-resumes).

## Persona
Primary: System (data model).
Secondary: End User (patrol movement is the most visible mechanic).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `state.patrol` object: `segments: [{x,y}, …]` (head first), `pendingGrowth: int`. Add `patrolReset(cx=16, cy=12, length=3, heading='right')` initialiser populating segments. Add `patrolStep()` called from `tick()`: read `state.pendingDirection ?? state.currentDirection`, commit it to `state.currentDirection`, compute new head, prepend to `segments`, then if `pendingGrowth > 0` decrement it (no truncate), else pop the tail. In Phase 1, `tick()` skips `patrolStep()` until `state.firstInputReceived` is true. Add a simple draw routine for segments (placeholder gray; P1-01 will replace). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### System
- [ ] `patrolReset()` produces 3 segments at the center cells (e.g. cells (16,12), (15,12), (14,12)) with head at (16,12) and `currentDirection = 'right'`.
- [ ] `patrolStep()` with `pendingDirection = 'right'` advances head from (16,12) to (17,12), drops tail (14,12) — patrol stays length 3.
- [ ] After `grow(1)` and one `patrolStep()`, length is 4 (tail retained).
- [ ] `patrolStep()` is not invoked while `firstInputReceived === false` (Phase 1 only — wait state).

### End User
- [ ] On page load, a 3-segment placeholder patrol is visible at the center.
- [ ] After pressing an arrow key, the patrol begins moving.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Standard linked-list-style snake body, but using a plain array. Head-first ordering simplifies collision checks later (just iterate `segments.slice(1)`).

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: open page, see 3 segments at center, press Right Arrow, watch patrol move right. Verify length stays 3 (no growth yet — FN-05 wires growth on pickup).
- Console probe: `state.patrol.segments.length` before/after a Right Arrow + few ticks.
- Regression: FN-02 loop, FN-03 input keep working.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~400 tokens (decide segment layout, reset / step / grow shape).
- Implement: ~3 turns, ~1500 tokens (model + tick wiring + placeholder draw).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~400 tokens (manual movement check + console probe).
- Total: ~5 turns, ~2300 tokens.
- Confidence: high (standard pattern; PRD §3.20 spec is explicit).
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (patrolReset, patrolStep, patrolGrow): segments array head-first, spawn at center (cx=16, cy=12) length 3 heading right, step advances head and either pops tail or decrements pendingGrowth.

title: FN-03: Input handler (arrows + WASD + direction reversal protection)
description:
# FN-03 — Input handler (arrows + WASD + direction reversal protection)

## Summary
Listen for `keydown` events on `window` and translate Arrow keys + WASD into a `pendingDirection` value (up/down/left/right). On each tick, FN-04 will read `pendingDirection` and apply it. Enforce two protections per PRD §3.18: (1) only one direction change applies per tick (no rapid double-input), (2) inputs that are the direct opposite of the current heading are rejected (not queued).

Per PRD §4.3 (controls); §3.18 (reversal protection); §3.39 (Phase 1 waits for first input — handled by FN-04 reading `firstInputReceived` flag this module sets).

## Persona
Primary: End User (player input is the primary interaction surface).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `state.pendingDirection` (null at start), `state.currentDirection` ('right' initial per §3.20), `state.firstInputReceived` (false until first valid arrow / WASD). Add `window.addEventListener('keydown', handleKey)`. `handleKey(e)` maps codes → direction strings, rejects opposite of `currentDirection`, sets `pendingDirection`, sets `firstInputReceived = true`. Also reserve key codes for P/Esc (pause — wired in FN-07), R/Space (redeploy — wired in FN-08), Shift (sprint — wired in P4-06); these dispatch to a stub for now. `e.preventDefault()` for arrow keys to stop page scroll. |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] ArrowUp/ArrowDown/ArrowLeft/ArrowRight set `pendingDirection` to up/down/left/right.
- [ ] W/A/S/D (case-insensitive) set the same directions.
- [ ] Pressing the direct opposite of `currentDirection` (e.g. Right while heading Left) leaves `pendingDirection` unchanged.
- [ ] Arrow keys do not scroll the page.
- [ ] First valid direction input sets `firstInputReceived = true`.

### System
- [ ] Multiple direction inputs between ticks resolve to the latest VALID input (not the latest input overall) — so a Right→Up→Down sequence while heading Left lands on Up (Right was rejected as opposite; Down is allowed).
- [ ] P, Esc, R, Space, Shift handlers exist (may stub `console.log` for now); FN-07 / FN-08 / P4-06 will replace.

## Patterns to Reuse
- Standard `keydown` mapping table. Code keys via `e.code` (`ArrowUp`, `KeyW`, etc.) for keyboard-layout independence.

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: open page, press each direction, observe `state.pendingDirection` via console. Try opposite-of-heading and verify rejection.
- Regression: FN-02 loop continues running.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (map keys + plan).
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1000 tokens (mapping + protections).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (console probe).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~1600 tokens.
- Confidence: high.
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (keydown handler): DIR_KEYS table for Arrows + WASD, pendingDirection set, isOpposite() rejection of direct reversals, P/Esc -> pause toggle, R/Space dispatched to redeploy only on patrol_lost state.

title: FN-02: Game loop and tick clock (pausable)
description:
# FN-02 — Game loop and tick clock (pausable)

## Summary
Implement the core game-loop module: `requestAnimationFrame`-driven render loop with a fixed-tick accumulator that decouples render rate from game-tick rate (per PRD §18 — "fixed-tick accumulator"). Tick rate is a state variable (cells/sec) consumable by other modules. A `paused` flag (toggled by FN-07) halts tick advancement without tearing down the render loop, so overlays still draw.

## Persona
Primary: System (engine core).
Secondary: End User (responsive movement and pause are user-perceived properties of this loop).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Edit | Add `state` object with `tickRate` (initial 6 cells/sec per PRD §3.16), `lastFrameTs`, `tickAccumulator`, `paused` (bool). Add `loop(ts)` function using `requestAnimationFrame` that computes `dt = ts - lastFrameTs`, accumulates into `tickAccumulator`, and calls a `tick()` hook (no-op until FN-04+) every `1000 / tickRate` ms while not paused. Render always runs (so paused overlay stays visible). Start with `requestAnimationFrame(loop)` once DOM is ready. |

## Acceptance Criteria
### System
- [ ] `state.tickRate` is mutable; changing it changes the tick frequency on the next frame.
- [ ] When `state.paused === true`, the `tick()` callback is NOT invoked but the render frame still runs (verify with a `console.log` probe inside `tick()` during a temporary pause).
- [ ] Browser tab visibility loss (which can spike `dt`) does not cause a flood of catch-up ticks. Cap accumulated dt at e.g. 250 ms per frame to avoid spiral-of-death.

### End User
- [ ] On a 60 Hz monitor with `tickRate = 6`, the `tick()` hook fires ~6 times per second (verifiable via a temporary frame-counter log).

## Patterns to Reuse
- Standard `requestAnimationFrame` + fixed-tick accumulator pattern. Reference: PRD §18 row "Game loop".

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: temporary `console.log` inside `tick()`; open devtools, observe tick rate matches `state.tickRate`. Toggle `state.paused = true` from the console, verify ticks stop.
- Regression: FN-01 scaffold still renders.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (review fixed-tick accumulator shape).
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1000 tokens (loop + state).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (console probe).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~1600 tokens.
- Confidence: high (textbook pattern).
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html (loop function): requestAnimationFrame loop with fixed-tick accumulator (1000/tickRate ms), MAX_DT_PER_FRAME cap to prevent catch-up storms after tab visibility loss, paused flag halts tick() while render keeps running.

title: FN-01: Bootstrap single-file HTML scaffold + canvas + HUD shell (panel/border frame)
description:
# FN-01 — Bootstrap single-file HTML scaffold + canvas + HUD shell

## Summary
Create the `snake_opus.html` file with inline HTML/CSS/JS scaffolding: a 640×480 px `<canvas>` element inside a thin dark-khaki border frame, an HUD container with placeholder regions (top-left score, top-center phase, top-right reinforcements), and the JS skeleton ready for the game loop and other modules. No external dependencies (no `<script src>`, no `<link rel="stylesheet" href>`, no CDN, no images). Per PRD §1, §3.14, §3.15, §13.1, §13.7.

## Persona
Primary: System (engine scaffold; the player never sees an empty scaffold without the rest of Phase 1 wired in).
Secondary: End User (the canvas frame and HUD shell *will* be visible at run start once the rest of Phase 1 lands).

## Changes Needed
| File | Action | What Changes |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `snake_opus.html` | Create | Single-file HTML document with `<head>` (title, inline `<style>`) and `<body>` (canvas wrapper div + canvas + HUD container + inline `<script>`). Set canvas `width="640"` `height="480"`. Background uses `#2b2d24`; border uses `#4a4632` (per PRD §6.2). HUD typography is monospace/stencil-feel (`font-family: 'Courier New', monospace; color: #e5e0c8`). |

## Acceptance Criteria
### End User
- [ ] Opening the file in Chrome / Edge / Firefox / Safari (modern versions) by double-click renders a dark-olive page with a 640×480 canvas centered, surrounded by a thin dark-khaki border.
- [ ] HUD container is visible with three placeholder regions (top-left, top-center, top-right) styled in parchment monospace text.
- [ ] No external HTTP requests when loading the file (verify in browser devtools Network tab).

### System
- [ ] File is one self-contained `.html` (no sibling JS/CSS/image files).
- [ ] `<script src=...>`, `<link rel="stylesheet" href=...>`, and any CDN URLs are absent.
- [ ] The `<canvas>` element exposes a `getContext('2d')` handle that subsequent modules can target.

## Patterns to Reuse
- File-naming + fallback per PRD §14: `snake_opus.html` (opus is the active model identifier per the system; if uncertain, fall back to `snake_sonnet.html`).
- Aesthetic per PRD §6.2 hex table (background `#2b2d24`, border `#4a4632`, HUD text `#e5e0c8`).

## Testing Strategy
- Manual: open `snake_opus.html` in Chrome, verify visual frame + HUD shell + zero network requests.
- Regression: no prior tests to keep green; this is the genesis file.

## Effort Forecast
- Plan: ~1 turn, ~400 tokens (re-read PRD §6.2 palette + §3.15 dimensions).
- Implement: ~2 turns, ~1500 tokens (write the scaffold; mostly CSS).
- Test: ~1 turn, ~300 tokens (manual open).
- Total: ~4 turns, ~2200 tokens.
- Confidence: high (purely scaffolding; no logic; PRD palette + dimensions are explicit).
close_reason: Implemented in examples/snake_opus.html: <head>/<style>/<body> scaffold, 640x480 canvas inside #4a4632 border frame, monospace HUD with score/phase/reinforcements regions, all inline (no external deps verified via grep).

title: Epic: Polish & Ship
description: Phase banners, reinforcement pickups + consumption, pause freezing world events, file-name + no-external-deps audit, whole-game AC. Per PRD §16.8.

title: Epic: Phase 5 — Sector Transfer
description: Open-ended sector progression via wormhole pairs, escalating bunker density and tempo, sector-respawn semantics. Per PRD §9.

title: Epic: Phase 4 — Bunker Run
description: Static bunker obstacles with flood-fill connectivity validation; sprint boost bonus. Gate score 350 → 700. Per PRD §8.

title: Epic: Phase 3 — Ambush
description: Hostile sweep events with telegraph and cut-at-strike-point math; Cover bonus. Gate score 150 → 350. Per PRD §7.

title: Epic: Phase 2 — Dawn Patrol
description: Camo palette unlocks, field rations, Scavenger Training upgrade, score multiplier. Gate score 50 → 150. Per PRD §6.

title: Epic: Phase 1 — Night Recon
description: Grayscale classic Snake under night-recon training mood. Score 0 → 50 gate. Per PRD §5.

title: Epic: Foundation
description: Single-file HTML scaffold, game loop, input, patrol model, supply spawn, HUD, hold position, PATROL LOST screen. Cross-cutting prerequisites for every phase. Per PRD §16.2.
